Q1: I kept my study routine pretty consistent throughout the course, dedicating about 8 hours a week spread across a few sessions. Being a visual learner, I'd usually start by watching the lecture videos and then create my own diagrams and mind maps to organize the concepts. I tried to keep things predictable because honestly, with six online courses running simultaneously, I needed that structure to stay on top of everything without burning out.
Q2: I mostly stuck to regular times each week—usually Tuesday and Thursday evenings and a longer session on Sunday mornings. That said, when big deadlines approached, I'd definitely shift things around and put in extra hours. My extracurriculars sometimes disrupted my schedule too, so I learned to be flexible while still maintaining that core routine I'd established.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check if new video lectures were posted or to see announcements from instructors. I also made it a habit to check the discussion boards a few times a week since I found the peer conversations helpful. Sometimes I'd just log in out of habit, almost like checking social media, just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became almost automatic—like part of my morning routine with coffee. I think it happened because I kept the tab pinned in my browser, and I'd see it every time I opened my laptop. The high attendance rate I maintained really came from making it feel normal rather than like a chore.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm such a visual learner—I must have watched over four hours' worth of content. I also found the discussion boards valuable because interacting with my peers helped me see concepts from different angles. The visual resources like diagrams and infographics in the course materials really clicked with how my brain processes information.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't engage much with some of the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources. They just didn't suit my learning style, and I'd often skim them quickly or skip them entirely if I felt I understood the concept from the videos. Some of the optional practice exercises also got pushed aside when time was tight, which probably explains my lower assignment completion rate.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to the exam material or when it presented information visually. If I could tell it would help me understand a concept better or prepare me for assessments, I was all in. I also valued activities that let me interact with classmates because those discussions often clarified things I was struggling with.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was nice, but that also meant they were the first things I'd cut when I felt stressed or overwhelmed. Required activities had this urgency that made me prioritize them no matter what. I sometimes wished I'd done more optional work, but with my motivation levels not always being high, I had to pick my battles.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded most of the video lectures and visual materials to my laptop so I could study offline, especially when I was commuting or in places with spotty internet. I also created tons of handwritten notes and visual summaries that I'd review constantly. A lot of my actual learning happened with these physical materials spread out on my desk, not while logged into the LMS.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd spend hours reviewing my hand-drawn diagrams and mind maps without touching the LMS at all. I'd also study with classmates in person, working through problems together using printed materials. Sometimes I'd watch a downloaded lecture video multiple times on my tablet while taking notes, and none of that activity would show up in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a video lecture once to download it, then watch it two or three more times offline while taking detailed visual notes. The same went for any diagrams or infographics—I'd save them and refer back to them constantly during my study sessions. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours of offline review.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my stress levels spiked and engagement became harder. Midway through the semester when all six of my courses had overlapping deadlines, I felt completely overwhelmed. During those times, I relied heavily on the mindfulness techniques I'd learned to manage my anxiety, but my motivation to go beyond the bare minimum really dropped.
Q13: The workload from managing six online courses simultaneously definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd stick to only the essential activities and skip anything optional. My extracurricular commitments also competed for my attention, so sometimes I'd do quick, surface-level engagement with materials just to check them off rather than diving deep.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd be watching a video or scrolling through discussion posts but my mind was completely elsewhere. When my stress was high, I'd go through the motions of engaging with materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have to go back later and actually focus on the content, but the LMS would show that initial unfocused session as engagement.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. My motivation wasn't always strong, and when I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid the more challenging tasks in favor of easier ones. The assignments I submitted weren't always my best work because I'd procrastinate and then rush through them, which probably explains the gap between my exam scores and assignment scores.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have periods of lower engagement and then intense bursts of activity as deadlines approached. They created a structure that helped me stay on track when my internal motivation was lacking. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread my studying more evenly, but they forced a certain rhythm to my engagement.
Q17: Feedback had a moderate effect on my engagement. When I received positive feedback, it gave me a small boost of motivation to keep going. However, when feedback was critical or when I didn't do as well as expected on an assignment, it sometimes made me want to disengage temporarily. I had to use mindfulness techniques to process that disappointment and get back on track.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me stay organized across all six of my online courses. Having a predictable pattern of video lectures followed by discussions and then assessments worked well for my need for routine. The structured approach meant I always knew what was coming next, which reduced some of my stress around planning.
Q19: The visual elements of the course design made engagement much easier for me—well-designed slides, diagrams, and video content really suited my learning style. What made it harder was when there were long text-heavy readings without visual breaks. The discussion board format was generally good, though sometimes threads got overwhelming to follow with so many participants.
Q20: I think students might show high LMS activity but submit little work for a few reasons. They could be like me—spending time watching videos and engaging with materials but struggling with the actual assignments due to low motivation or feeling overwhelmed. Or they might be exploring content out of interest without prioritizing the formal submissions. The engagement is real, but it doesn't always translate to completed work.
Q21: This actually resonates with my experience somewhat. I performed well on exams despite not completing all assignments because a lot of my learning happened offline. Someone could watch a video once, download it, study extensively offline, and ace the exam while showing minimal LMS activity. The system can't capture the hours spent with printed notes or hand-drawn diagrams.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, which resources students access, and participation in discussions. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and the emotional state of the student. They can't tell if someone is genuinely learning or just going through the motions, and they can't capture learning that happens with downloaded materials or peer study groups.
Q23: For me, being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and connect it to what I already know, not just passively consuming content. It means showing up consistently, participating in discussions with thoughtful contributions, and putting real effort into learning even when motivation is low. Engagement is about mental presence and investment in my own learning, whether or not the LMS can see it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent creating visual study materials by hand or reviewing downloaded content offline. It wouldn't capture the stress I managed through mindfulness practices or how my low motivation affected the quality of my engagement even when I was logged in. It also can't show the peer study sessions I had outside the platform or how much my extracurricular commitments shaped when and how I could engage with coursework.
